findings.description: Inverted expiry check: `_is_expired` returns `age < self.ttl`, which is True for FRESH entries and False for EXPIRED ones — the exact opposite of its name and intent.

Because `_is_expired` is inverted, `get` deletes still-fresh L1 entries (line 54) and returns stale/expired entries as cache hits (lines 56-57), so TTL eviction never happens and the cache serves stale data indefinitely while defeating the L1 tier.

Even with corrected comparison the boundary is ambiguous: `age < ttl` vs the intended `age >= ttl` differ at exactly age==ttl; the fix must choose `>=` (expire at/after ttl) to match `setex` TTL semantics, otherwise L1 and L2 expire one tick apart.
`pickle.loads` on bytes read from Redis is an arbitrary-code-execution sink if the Redis value is ever attacker-influenced or poisoned — flagging in passing; defer detail to critic-security.
`self._local` has no max-size or eviction policy, so the in-process L1 dict grows unbounded (entries are only trimmed on read access), a memory-growth risk for a long-running web service.

Concurrency hazard: `_local`/`stats` are mutated without locking in a service that puts an in-process dict in front of Redis; line 54 uses `del self._local[key]` (raises KeyError if another thread already evicted) whereas other call sites correctly use `.pop(..., None)`, making this a check-then-act race.

`get`/`get_or_compute` use `is not None` as the hit sentinel, so a legitimately cached value of `None` is indistinguishable from a miss — `compute()` re-runs every call and a cached `None` is never served.

`stats.local_entries` is only refreshed in `set`; `get`'s L1 population (line 61) and eviction (line 54) leave it stale, so the field is internally inconsistent (export_stats masks this by recomputing len() directly).

Fixture `RedisClient.setex` ignores `ttl_seconds` and never expires L2 entries; flagged as a documented stub ('network elided'), so real Redis would honor TTL — noted only because, as written, the module has no working expiry at either tier.

`warm` returns `len(entries)` (entries requested) but its docstring claims 'number of entries written'; harmless here since `set` cannot partially succeed, but the contract wording overstates the guarantee.
